planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; nick the seed coat before sowing to improve germination. Transplant after all danger of frost is past. In zones 9–11 it grows as a perennial and can be direct sown in spring.

directions: Start indoors in small pots and transplant to trellises, fences, or hanging baskets after last frost; black-eyed Susan vine twines quickly and produces a cheerful abundance of 1.5–2 inch five-petaled flowers in warm golden-orange, yellow, and white shades with a striking dark brown or black center eye. Unlike most vining plants, it performs well in containers and hanging baskets, with stems draping 3–6 feet. It tolerates partial shade better than most annuals and blooms from early summer until frost in northern gardens. In tropical climates it grows as a woody perennial. Pinch growing tips when planting to encourage branching and more flowers. Excellent on a mailbox post, small obelisk, or trellised wall where its cheerful flowers can be appreciated up close.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost, soaking seeds overnight first; cypress vine grows quickly from direct seeding and does not transplant well due to its taproot. In zones 9–11 it self-sows prolifically and may return year after year.

directions: Direct sow soaked seeds at the base of a trellis, fence, or shrub after last frost; cypress vine is a fast-climbing morning glory relative with elegant, feathery, finely cut foliage that resembles cypress or asparagus fern and is totally unlike the coarse leaves of other Ipomoea species. The bright scarlet (or white in some cultivars) star-shaped flowers are intensely attractive to hummingbirds and are produced prolifically from summer until frost. It climbs by twining to 10 feet or more. In the South and Gulf Coast it self-sows to return reliably each year. The hybrid 'Cardinal Vine' (I. x multifida, different from the 'cardinal-climber' cultivar) produces similar but slightly larger red flowers with lobed rather than feathery leaves.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, nicking or soaking seeds first; Spanish flag grows quickly and appreciates a head start. In zones 9–11 it can be direct sown in warm soil after last frost and grows as a perennial.

directions: Start indoors and transplant to a sunny trellis or fence after last frost; Spanish flag is an unusual and showy Ipomoea vine producing one-sided clusters of tubular flowers that dramatically change color as they age — opening bright scarlet red at the tip of the cluster, fading through orange to yellow to white at the base, so that a single flower cluster simultaneously displays four different colors like a living color gradient. The palm-shaped, deeply lobed leaves are attractive even without flowers. The plant climbs quickly to 10 feet or more and blooms from midsummer through frost. It is an outstanding plant for adding tropical flair to a trellis, arbor, or fence and the constant succession of multicolored flower clusters is endlessly interesting.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, sowing seeds on their edges 1/2 inch deep to avoid rotting; cup and saucer vine is a vigorous climber that needs a head start and should be set out after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Start seeds on their edges in moist compost and transplant to a strong trellis or arbor after last frost; cup and saucer vine is a fast-growing Mexican native that can cover 15–20 feet of trellis in a single season, producing an abundance of large, bell-shaped flowers with a prominent ribbed green 'saucer' calyx that makes each flower look like a flower-in-a-cup. The flowers open green-white, then rapidly age to deep purple, so the plant carries both colors simultaneously in an interesting contrast. They are intensely fragrant, with a sweet musky scent. Tendrils at the leaf tips cling to virtually anything with great strength. In zones 9–11 it grows as a perennial woody vine and becomes even more vigorous in its second year.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring before new growth or in early fall; Primula sieboldii is winter-dormant and goes completely underground from July through February, so mark planting sites to avoid disturbing the invisible rhizomes.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese star primrose is a refined shade garden perennial from Japan producing beautifully fringed or ruffled flowers in pink, lavender, white, and magenta in early spring — the delicate, often lacy petals give a completely different character from the simple round flowers of common primrose. The textured, crinkled leaves emerge in early spring alongside the flowers and persist through early summer, then the plant disappears completely below ground until the following spring. This summer dormancy makes it well-adapted to dry woodland conditions. The Japanese have cultivated hundreds of named cultivars ('sieboldii') for over 400 years as a refined collector's plant with extraordinary variety in flower form and color.
